abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Summary of Insurance Requirements for Issaquah Court Condominiums.
abstracts.subtitle: The Insurance provisions from the Declaration, Bylaws, and Articles of Incorporation outline coverage requirements for both the Association and individual unit owners. Below is a structured summary:

blocks.heading: 1.1.1 Property Insurance (Fire & Casualty)
blocks.text: Covers the Units (excluding personal property) and Common/ Limited Common Elements for full replacement value, including improvements/betterments installed by Unit Owners. Deductibles and exclusions may apply.

blocks.heading: 1.1.2 General Liability Insurance
blocks.text: Protects the Association, Board, Declarant, and Unit Owners against bodily injury or property damage arising from use or ownership of Common Elements. Minimum limits include $1 million per occurrence for personal injury and $50,000 per accident for property damage.

blocks.heading: 1.1.3 Directors & Officers Insurance
blocks.text: Coverage for acts or omissions of the Board and officers, with liability limited provided actions are in good faith and within the scope of authority.

blocks.heading: 1.1.4 Fidelity/Crime Insurance
blocks.text: Protects against theft or dishonesty involving Association funds.

blocks.heading: 1.1.5 Workers’ Compensation Insurance
blocks.text: Required per applicable laws for any employees or contractors engaged by the Association.

blocks.heading: 1.1.6 Personal Property Insurance
blocks.text: Covers Association-owned equipment and furnishings against fire, theft, or other losses.

blocks.heading: 1.1.7 Additional Insurance
blocks.text: The Board may secure other coverage deemed necessary or advisable, including flood or earthquake insurance, depending on location and risk.

blocks.heading: 2.1.1 HO-6 Insurance (Strongly recommended)
blocks.text: Unit Owners may obtain additional coverage at their expense for personal property, interior improvements, and liability.

blocks.heading: 2.1.2 Loss Assessment Coverage
blocks.text: Not explicitly required, but recommended to cover Association-assessed deductibles or shared damage costs.

blocks.heading: 2.1.3 Owner Insurance Restrictions
blocks.text: No policy may impair or reduce the Association’s coverage. Owners must notify the Board of any improvements over $1,000 and submit a copy of any individual property coverage outside personal property within 30 days.

blocks.heading: 3.1 Association’s Role in Claims & Payouts
items: Insurance proceeds are held in trust for reconstruction and cannot be distributed to Unit Owners unless funds exceed the cost of repairs or reconstruction is not pursued.
The Association has the authority to settle insurance claims on behalf of all insured parties.

blocks.heading: 3.2 Mortgagee Protections
items: Policies must include standard mortgagee clauses.
Insurance rights of lenders are not invalidated by Owner or Board negligence.

blocks.heading: 3.3 Owner Responsibility for Damage
blocks.text: Owners are responsible for damage caused by themselves, their tenants, guests, or pets to Common or Limited Common Elements, and costs may be specially assessed.

blocks.heading: 4. Insurance Policy Requirements & Compliance
items: Annual Review: The Board must annually review insurance adequacy with professional consultants.
Carrier Rating: All policies must be obtained through insurers rated Class B or better by Best’s Guide or equivalent.
Waivers & Subrogation: Policies must waive subrogation rights against Unit Owners and ensure coverage is not voided by Owner conduct.
Primary Coverage: Association policies are considered primary in the event of overlapping Owner insurance.
Notice Requirements: Any cancellation must be communicated to the Board and interested parties at least 30 days in advance.

takeaways: The Association maintains comprehensive insurance on the physical structures, liability, and Board operations.
Unit Owners are encouraged to carry HO-6 and Loss Assessment policies and must notify the Board of substantial improvements.
Claims are managed by the Board, with mortgagees given high protection.
Policy compliance and adequate coverage are overseen by annual professional reviews.

abstracts.title: Maintenance Responsibilities
abstracts.desc: Maintenance Responsibilities Summary for Issaquah Court.
abstracts.subtitle: From Articles of Incorporation, Bylaws, and Declaration of Issaquah Court.

blocks.heading: Maintenance Responsibilities Overview
blocks.text: The governing documents outline the division of maintenance duties between the Association and Unit Owners to ensure building functionality, property value, and community integrity.

blocks.heading: Unit Owner Responsibilities
blocks.text: Each Unit Owner is responsible for the maintenance, repair, and replacement of the following:

items.text: All interior components within the Unit boundaries, including:
sub: Fixtures, appliances, and equipment serving only that Unit
Interior plumbing, electrical systems, and HVAC devices exclusive to the Unit

items.text: Exclusive use areas designated as Limited Common Elements, such as:
sub: Balconies, patios, decks, and assigned parking stalls
Windows and exterior doors serving only one Unit

items: Clean-up after guests, tenants, and contractors if spills or damage occur in common areas

blocks.heading: Owners are also financially liable for:
items: Damage caused by residents, guests, or pets to Common or Limited Common Elements
Remediation of oil leaks in garages, trash left in Common Areas, and related infractions

blocks.heading: Association Responsibilities (HOA)
blocks.text: The Association is responsible for maintenance, repair, and replacement of:

sub: Building structure: roofs, exterior walls, siding, and foundations
Common utilities: plumbing, HVAC, fire sprinklers, electrical systems outside Unit boundaries
Elevators, stairwells, lobbies, landscaping, parking garage, sidewalks
Fire control panels, alarms, and monitoring

blocks.text: The Association also coordinates professional services, hazard insurance, and utility contracts on behalf of all Units (allocated proportionally).

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: In the case of an emergency where immediate action is required to prevent property damage or safety hazards, the Association has the right to:
items: Enter Units for inspection and remediation (with notice when possible)
Initiate urgent repairs to preserve building safety and structural integrity
Recover costs from the responsible Unit Owner when damage originates from the Unit or is due to negligence

blocks.heading: Assessment & Funding for Maintenance
blocks.text: Maintenance obligations are funded through:
items: Monthly Assessments levied on all Unit Owners (based on percentage interest)
Special Assessments for major repairs not covered in the operating budget
Cost Allocation Matrix which determines shared responsibility between Residential and Commercial Units for specific systems and areas

blocks.text: Limited Common Element costs may be assessed solely to benefiting Units.

blocks.heading: Consequences for Neglecting Maintenance
blocks.text: Failure to meet maintenance responsibilities or reimburse the Association for damage can result in:
items: Fines, legal action, or special assessments
Suspension of voting rights or use of amenities
Lien placement and foreclosure proceedings for nonpayment of maintenance-related assessments

takeaways: Unit Owners are responsible for maintaining everything inside their Unit and exclusive use areas.
The Association maintains shared building systems, infrastructure, and common spaces.
Costs are allocated based on use and benefit as outlined in the Settlement Agreement Matrix.
Neglect or damage may result in financial liability, fines, or legal enforcement.

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.subtitle: From Articles of Incorporation, Bylaws, and Declaration of Issaquah Court Association.

blocks.heading: Purpose of the ACC
blocks.text: To preserve the architectural integrity and uniform appearance of Issaquah Court. The Board of Directors is empowered to regulate any modifications or decorations that affect the Common Elements, Limited Common Elements, or building exterior to maintain consistent aesthetic standards and prevent impacts to structural integrity, mechanical systems, or neighboring units. (Declaration §§ 11.7.2, 11.9, 11.14, 11.15)

blocks.heading: Types of Changes Requiring ACC Approval
blocks.text: All alterations or modifications to a Unit—including changes to flooring, plumbing, electrical systems, exterior elements (such as windows), or any modifications impacting Common or Limited Common Elements—require prior written approval from the Board of Directors.
items: Any alteration to Limited Common Elements (e.g., balconies, decks, parking)
Exterior modifications, including paint, awnings, screen doors, planters, and visible equipment
Interior changes affecting walls, ceilings, floors, or fixtures
Any change affecting structural integrity, or common mechanical/electrical systems
Changes to window coverings visible from the outside

blocks.heading: Approval Process
items: Owners must submit a written request to the Board of Directors
The Board evaluates the impact on aesthetics, structural systems, and other units
Approval is required before work begins
For certain alterations (e.g., combining units), plans/specifications must be submitted, and the Board must respond within 30 days or the request is deemed approved
(Declaration §§ 11.14.3, 11.15; Rules § 2)

blocks.heading: Restrictions & Guidelines
items: No changes allowed that interfere with structural integrity or building systems
All window coverings must be uniform (white or off-white)
Screen doors must be uniform and pre-approved
Contractors must follow Association rules, including noise limits and debris removal
Garbage containers may not be used for construction debris
(Rules §§ 2, 8)

blocks.heading: Consequences for Non-Compliance
items: Unapproved work may be subject to removal at the owner’s expense
The Board may issue a fine, and owners may request a Review Panel hearing
The Review Panel consists of 3 non-Board members and must deliver a written decision within 5 days
Decisions of the Review Panel are final, barring fraud or procedural error
(Rules § 8, Review Panel §§ g.i–g.vi)

takeaways: All exterior and structural changes require Board approval in writing
Changes that affect common systems or appearance are strictly regulated
Uniformity of window coverings and screen doors is mandatory
Owners are liable for unauthorized modifications or damage
The Review Panel provides due process for fines or violations

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Summary of Board of Directors Provisions.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors serves as the main decision-making body for the condominium. Its authority covers rule enforcement, financial management, maintenance, and legal matters. The Developer initially appoints the Board, but once unit owners take control, they elect directors to oversee operations. Board members serve without compensation and must act in the best interest of all unit owners.

blocks.heading: Structure & Composition
items: The Board shall consist of three (3) persons who govern the affairs of the Association.
During the initial phase, the Declarant appoints a Temporary Board. Once control transitions to the unit owners, they elect the Board pursuant to the Declaration.

blocks.heading: Election & Term

sub: After 25% of units are conveyed, at least 25% of the Board must be elected by unit owners (non-Declarant). Full owner control and election of all Board members occurs within 30 days after the Declarant Control Period ends.

items: Term Length: Directors serve two (2) year staggered terms—two elected in even-numbered years, and three in odd-numbered years.
Filling Vacancies: Vacancies are filled per the Declaration. Directors elected to fill vacancies serve the remainder of the term.

blocks.heading: Removal of Directors
blocks.text: Directors may be removed with or without cause by a two-thirds (2/3) vote of the Association, except for those appointed by the Declarant during the Declarant Control Period.

blocks.heading: Board Meetings & Decision-Making
items: The Board must hold at least two regular meetings per fiscal year with three days’ notice. Special meetings can be called by the President or upon request by a Board majority.
A majority of Directors constitutes a quorum. Actions are decided by a majority vote at meetings with quorum.

blocks.heading: Officers of the Board
blocks.text: Officers include a President, Secretary, and Treasurer, elected annually by the Board from its members. A Vice President, Assistant Treasurer, or Assistant Secretary may be appointed if needed.
Officers serve at the Board’s pleasure and may be removed at any time with or without cause.

blocks.heading: Board Committees & Delegation
items: The Board may create committees of Directors to manage certain functions, provided the Board retains final responsibility.

The Board may also appoint non-director committees, which must include at least one Board member. Duties can be delegated to a manager, except those involving election of officers, bylaw amendments, or rule adoption.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
items: No compensation is provided to Directors for their service.
Reasonable compensation may be paid to officers or members performing substantial services.

blocks.heading: Decision-Making Authority & Rule Enforcement
items: The Board enacts rules, enforces regulations, manages finances, and represents the Association in contracts and legal matters.
The Board is empowered to levy assessments, initiate foreclosure for delinquencies, and enforce community rules.

blocks.heading: Board and Mortgagee Rights
blocks.text: The Board must maintain mortgagee protections and comply with provisions in the Declaration related to lender notifications and voting rights.

blocks.heading: Amendments & Board Authority Changes
blocks.text: Amendments to the Bylaws require unit owners holding 60% or more of the total votes to be present, in person or by proxy, at a duly called meeting.

takeaways: The Board holds comprehensive authority to operate and maintain the condominium in accordance with its governing documents.
It is responsible for compliance, financial management, legal action, and enforcement of rules, with powers expanding upon transfer of control from the Declarant.
Directors serve without compensation and may be removed by two-thirds vote.
Amendments to Bylaws require 60% of total votes present at a duly called meeting.

abstracts.title: Resale Certificate
abstracts.desc: Resale Certificate Summary for Issaquah Court Condominiums.
abstracts.subtitle: A Resale Certificate is required for the sale or transfer of any unit within Issaquah Court Condominiums. It provides critical financial and governance disclosures to prospective buyers. Below is a consolidation of all relevant sections regarding the Resale Certificate from the governing documents, with section references for easy verification.

blocks.heading: Purpose & Legal Requirement
blocks.text: The Resale Certificate serves as a legal disclosure to prospective purchasers, in accordance with RCW 64.34.425, and provides essential information about the financial, operational, and governance aspects of the Association. It ensures that buyers are fully informed before completing a purchase.

blocks.heading: Who Prepares the Resale Certificate?
blocks.text: The Association, through its managing agent or board, is responsible for maintaining records that enable compliance with Resale Certificate requests.

blocks.heading: 3. Required Information in the Resale Certificate
blocks.text: The Resale Certificate must include:

blocks.heading: 3.1 Financial Disclosures
items: Operating budget and reserves
Special assessments and delinquencies
Financial statements, including annual audits if applicable
Fees associated with unit ownership

blocks.heading: 3.2 Legal & Governance Disclosures
items: Governing documents (Declaration, Bylaws, Rules)
Any pending litigation
Insurance coverage summary for Association and Common Elements
Minutes of the last board meetings

blocks.heading: 3.3 Property & Maintenance Disclosures
items: Responsibility matrix for maintenance
Status of capital improvement projects
Maintenance history of the building and unit (if available)
Copies of warranties for Common Elements still in effect

blocks.heading: Buyer’s Right to Cancel
blocks.text: Under RCW 64.34.420, a buyer may cancel a purchase within seven days after receiving the Resale Certificate. This statutory right protects purchasers who receive materially adverse disclosures.

blocks.heading: 5. Fees & Seller Responsibility
blocks.text: The seller typically pays the Resale Certificate preparation fee, which is charged by the Association or its management company. This fee must be reasonable and is often set in the Rules or by policy.

blocks.heading: 6. Importance of Compliance
blocks.text: Failure to provide an accurate or timely Resale Certificate may delay or invalidate a real estate transaction. It is also a requirement under Washington State law that the Association retain detailed records sufficient for resale compliance.

takeaways: Resale Certificates are legally required for unit sales.
The Association must maintain records to support certificate preparation.
Buyers have a 7-day statutory cancellation period.
Disclosures include financials, rules, insurance, and pending claims.
Sellers are typically responsible for certificate fees.
